---
title: "sharks"
output: html_document
date: "2025-10-22"
---
```{r}
 library(readxl)
library(dplyr)
library(tidyverse)
Blood_count1 <- read_excel("smears_analysis_r.xlsx")
```

```{r}
Blood_count1 <- Blood_count1 %>%
  dplyr::rename(
    `total_cell` = `[Cells] c/ml`
  )
```

```{r}
Blood_count1 <- Blood_count1 %>%
  dplyr::rename(
    `Shark_Id` = `Sharks Id`
  )
```


```{r}
cat("Original rows:", nrow(Blood_count1), "\n")
cat("Cleaned rows:", nrow(Blood_data_clean), "\n")
head(Blood_data_clean)
```


```{r}
library(tidyverse)
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
boxplot_outliers <- ggplot(Blood_data_clean, aes(x = factor(week), y = total_cell)) +
  # The geom_boxplot() function creates the boxplot and automatically plots outliers as points.
  geom_boxplot(fill = "lightblue", color = "darkblue") +
  # Customize labels and theme for a professional look
  labs(
    title = "Outlier Check: Total Cell Count by Week",
    x = "Week Number",
    y = "Total Cell Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# 3. Display the plot
print(boxplot_outliers)
```

```{r}
boxplot_group_outliers <- ggplot(Blood_data_clean, aes(x = Groupe, y = total_cell)) +
  # Use geom_boxplot() to create the plot and display outliers (dots)
  geom_boxplot(fill = "lightcoral", color = "darkred") +
  # Customize labels and theme
  labs(
    title = "Outlier Check: Total Cell Count by Groupe",
    x = "Groupe",
    y = "Total Cell Count"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

# Display the plot
print(boxplot_group_outliers)
```
```{r}
Blood_data_clean$week_factor <- factor(Blood_data_clean$week)
library(car)
# 5. Run the Levene's Test using the new factor variable
levene_result_week <- leveneTest(total_cell ~ week_factor, data = Blood_data_clean)
print(levene_result_week)
```

```{r}
library(car)

# 2. Run Levene's Test for 'total_cell' across 'Groupe'
levene_two_way <- leveneTest(total_cell ~ Groupe * factor(week), 
                             data = Blood_data_clean)
print(levene_two_way)
```
```{r}
library(dplyr)
library(ggplot2)
library(Rmisc)
data_summary <- summarySE(
  Blood_data_clean, 
  measurevar = "total_cell", 
  groupvars = c("Groupe", "week_factor")
)

# 3. Create the grouped bar plot
# We use 'fill=week_factor' to color the bars by week, and 'facet_wrap(~Groupe)' 
# to show a separate panel for each Groupe.
barplot_grouped <- ggplot(data_summary, aes(x = week_factor, y = total_cell, fill = week_factor)) +
  geom_bar(stat = "identity", position = position_dodge(0.9)) + # Bar for the mean
  geom_errorbar(
    aes(ymin = total_cell - se, ymax = total_cell + se), # Error bars for +/- SE
    width = 0.2, 
    position = position_dodge(0.9)
  ) +
  facet_wrap(~ Groupe) + # Creates separate panels for each Groupe
  labs(
    title = "Mean Total Cell Count by Week, Grouped by Groupe",
    x = "Week Number",
    y = "Mean Total Cell Count",
    fill = "Week"
  ) +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(legend.position = "bottom")

# 4. Display the plot
print(barplot_grouped)
```

```{r}
library(ggplot2)

# Create the QQ plot, faceted by the interaction of Groupe and week
qq_plots <- ggplot(Blood_data_clean, aes(sample = total_cell)) +
  # Create a separate panel for every unique Groupe/week combination
  facet_grid(Groupe ~ week_factor) +
  # Add the plot points and the normality line
  stat_qq() + 
  stat_qq_line() +
  labs(
    title = "QQ Plots for total_cell: Normality Check by Groupe and Week",
    x = "Theoretical Quantiles",
    y = "Sample Quantiles"
  ) +
  theme_bw()

print(qq_plots)
```

```{r}
# 1. Create a new log-transformed variable
Blood_data_clean$log_total_cell <- log(Blood_data_clean$total_cell)

# 2. Re-check the assumptions on the new variable
# Re-run Levene's Test:
library(car)
leveneTest(log_total_cell ~ factor(week), data = Blood_data_clean)
```

```{r}
library(dplyr)

Blood_data_clean <- Blood_data_clean %>%
  mutate(log_total_cell = log10(total_cell)) 

# Check the first few rows to ensure the new column was created
print(head(Blood_data_clean))
library(car)
levene_log_groupe <- leveneTest(log_total_cell ~ Groupe, data = Blood_data_clean)
print(levene_log_groupe)
levene_log_week <- leveneTest(log_total_cell ~ factor(week), data = Blood_data_clean)
print(levene_log_week)
```
```{r}
qq_plots_log <- Blood_data_clean %>%
  # Convert week to a factor for proper grouping
  mutate(week_factor = factor(week)) %>%
  
  ggplot(aes(sample = log_total_cell)) +
  # Create a separate panel for every unique Groupe/week combination
  facet_grid(Groupe ~ week_factor) +
  # Add the plot points and the normality line
  stat_qq() + 
  stat_qq_line() +
  labs(
    title = "QQ Plots for Log(Total Cell Count): Normality Check by Groupe and Week",
    x = "Theoretical Quantiles (Normal Distribution)",
    y = "Sample Quantiles (Log(Total Cell Count))"
  ) +
  theme_bw()

print(qq_plots_log)
```
```{r}
# 1. Ensure 'week' is correctly formatted as a factor for the ANOVA
Blood_data_clean$week_factor <- factor(Blood_data_clean$week)

# 2. Run the Standard Two-Way ANOVA
# The formula 'Groupe * week_factor' includes the main effects AND the interaction
anova_result <- aov(log_total_cell ~ Groupe * week_factor, data = Blood_data_clean)

# 3. View the ANOVA summary table
summary(anova_result)
```


```{r}
Blood_data_clean$interaction_group <- interaction(Blood_data_clean$Groupe, Blood_data_clean$week_factor)

# 2. Run the ANOVA using the new interaction term (Alternative way to test interaction)
# Note: You can also use the original anova_result object, but this is sometimes cleaner
interaction_model <- aov(log_total_cell ~ interaction_group, data = Blood_data_clean)

# 3. Perform Tukey's HSD post-hoc test
tukey_result <- TukeyHSD(interaction_model)

# 4. View the results (focus on the 'p adj' column for significance)
print(tukey_result)
```

```{r}
library(emmeans)
# Get the estimated marginal means (which are the cell means)
emmeans_output <- emmeans(anova_result, ~ week_factor | Groupe)

# Plot the interaction
plot(emmeans_output, type = "response") # Use type="response" if you want to see the means on the original scale (though the test was on the log scale)
```

```{r}
library(lme4) # Package for Mixed-Effects Models
library(lmerTest) # For calculating p-values in lme4 models

# Ensure factors are correct (using your cleaned data)
Blood_data_clean$week_factor <- factor(Blood_data_clean$week)
Blood_data_clean$Sex_factor <- factor(Blood_data_clean$Sex)
Blood_data_clean$Groupe_factor <- factor(Blood_data_clean$Groupe)
Blood_data_clean$Shark_Id_factor <- factor(Blood_data_clean$`Shark_Id`)
```

```{r}
library(MuMIn)
library(DHARMa)
glmm_model <- lmer(
  log_total_cell ~ Groupe_factor * week_factor + Sex_factor +  (1 | Shark_Id_factor), 
  data = Blood_data_clean
)

# View the full results (lmerTest calculates the p-values)
summary(glmm_model)
r.squaredGLMM(glmm_model)
simulation_output <- simulateResiduals(fittedModel = glmm_model, n = 250) 

# 2. Plot the main diagnostic checks
# This generates a 3-panel plot: Uniformity, QQ-plot, and Dispersion check
plot(simulation_output)
```
```{r}
emmeans_week <- emmeans(glmm_model, specs = "week_factor")

# 2. Define the contrast vector. Assuming 'week_factor' levels are ordered: 4, 6, 8.
#    To test (Week 8) minus (Week 6): c(0, -1, 1)
w8_vs_w6_contrast <- c(0, -1, 1)

# 3. Perform the specific contrast test (CORRECTED SYNTAX)
contrast_w8_vs_w6 <- contrast(emmeans_week, 
                              method = list("W8_vs_W6" = w8_vs_w6_contrast), 
                              adjust = "none")

# 4. Print the result
print(contrast_w8_vs_w6)
```



```{r}
library(emmeans)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# The formula '~ week_factor * Groupe_factor' tells emmeans to average over all other fixed effects (Sex_factor)
emmeans_output_avg_sex <- emmeans(glmm_model, 
                                  ~ week_factor * Groupe_factor)

# 2. Convert EMMs to a data frame for plotting
emmeans_df_avg_sex <- as.data.frame(summary(emmeans_output_avg_sex))

# 3. Create the Plot
line_plot_avg_sex <- ggplot(emmeans_df_avg_sex, 
       aes(x = week_factor, y = emmean, 
           color = Groupe_factor, 
           group = Groupe_factor)) +
  
  # Add lines for trends over time
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  
  # Add points for individual means
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  
  # Add error bars based on the Standard Error (SE)
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = emmean - SE, ymax = emmean + SE), 
                width = 0.1) +
  
  # Custom labels
  labs(
    title = "Estimated Mean Log(Total Cell Count) Over Time by Group",
    subtitle = "Averaged across Male and Female",
    y = "Estimated Marginal Mean of Log(Total Cell Count)",
    x = "Week",
    color = "Group"
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Contrôle" = "darkgrey", "Summer" = "darkgreen", "Winter" = "darkblue")) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# 4. Save and Display the plot
ggsave("avg_sex_emmeans_plot.png", plot = line_plot_avg_sex, width = 7, height = 5)
print(line_plot_avg_sex)
```



```{r}
library(lmerTest)
library(MuMIn)
library(DHARMa)
library(dplyr) # Still needed for the filtering step

# --- STEP 1: Filter the data for the Female-Only Model ---
# This assumes the 'Eggs_Female' column is already correctly defined in 'Blood_data_clean'
female_data_clean <- Blood_data_clean %>%
  filter(Sex_factor == "Female") %>%
  # IMPORTANT: Filter out NA in Eggs_Female (rows where Eggs was NA)
  filter(!is.na(Eggs_Female))

# --- STEP 2: Fit the Female-Only LMM ---
glmm_model_females_eggs <- lmer(
  # Sex is dropped as it is a constant ('Female') in the filtered data
  log_total_cell ~ Groupe_factor * week_factor + Eggs_Female + (1 | Shark_Id_factor), 
  data = female_data_clean
)

# 3. View the full results 
summary(glmm_model_females_eggs)
r.squaredGLMM(glmm_model_females_eggs)

# 4. Check residuals
simulation_output_females_eggs <- simulateResiduals(fittedModel = glmm_model_females_eggs, n = 250)
plot(simulation_output_females_eggs)
```


```{r}
library(emmeans)
library(ggplot2)
library(dplyr)

# ASSUMPTION: glmm_model_females_eggs is your female-only LMM object.

# 1. Calculate EMMs for the full three-way combination (Group * Week * Eggs)
emmeans_output_females <- emmeans(glmm_model_females_eggs, 
                                  ~ week_factor | Groupe_factor * Eggs_Female)

# 2. Convert EMMs to a data frame for plotting
emmeans_df_females <- as.data.frame(summary(emmeans_output_females))

# 3. Create the Plot
line_plot_females <- ggplot(emmeans_df_females, 
       aes(x = week_factor, y = emmean, 
           color = Groupe_factor, 
           group = Groupe_factor)) +
  
  # Add lines for trends over time
  geom_line(linewidth = 1) +
  
  # Add points for individual means, with shape based on egg status
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  
  # Add error bars based on the Standard Error (SE)
  geom_errorbar(aes(ymin = emmean - SE, ymax = emmean + SE), 
                width = 0.1) +
  
  # Separate the plot into panels based on Egg Status (Yes/No)
  facet_wrap(~ Eggs_Female) + 
  
  # Custom labels
  labs(
    title = "Estimated Marginal Mean Log(Total Cell Count) in Females",
    y = "Estimated Mean of Log(Total Cell Count)",
    x = "Week",
    color = "Group"
  ) +
  theme_bw() +
  theme(
    plot.title = element_text(hjust = 0.5, size = 14),
    axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 0, hjust = 0.5),
    legend.position = "bottom"
  )

# 4. Save and Display the plot
ggsave("female_eggs_emmeans_plot.png", plot = line_plot_females, width = 8, height = 5)
print(line_plot_females)
```
